verses.text: Moab is counselled to send tribute lambs to Zion's ruler and to shelter the outcasts of Judah. Mercy is offered through submission. The way of safety for the proud is to bow and show kindness to God's people.

Moab is counselled to send tribute lambs to Zion's ruler and to shelter the outcasts of Judah. Mercy is offered through submission. The way of safety for the proud is to bow and show kindness to God's people.

Moab is counselled to send tribute lambs to Zion's ruler and to shelter the outcasts of Judah. Mercy is offered through submission. The way of safety for the proud is to bow and show kindness to God's people.

If Moab shelters them, then 'in mercy shall the throne be established,' one sitting in truth and judging righteously. The promise looks to a just, merciful king. This points to Christ, whose throne is founded on truth and grace.

If Moab shelters them, then 'in mercy shall the throne be established,' one sitting in truth and judging righteously. The promise looks to a just, merciful king. This points to Christ, whose throne is founded on truth and grace.
But the pride of Moab is great, his boasts vain, so his fields and vines languish and are torn down. Pride forfeits mercy. The arrogance that refuses to submit brings desolation on the land.
But the pride of Moab is great, his boasts vain, so his fields and vines languish and are torn down. Pride forfeits mercy. The arrogance that refuses to submit brings desolation on the land.
But the pride of Moab is great, his boasts vain, so his fields and vines languish and are torn down. Pride forfeits mercy. The arrogance that refuses to submit brings desolation on the land.
Isaiah weeps for the wasted vine of Sibmah, his heart sounding 'like an harp for Moab.' Grief mingles with judgment. The fall of even an enemy nation moves a compassionate heart.
Isaiah weeps for the wasted vine of Sibmah, his heart sounding 'like an harp for Moab.' Grief mingles with judgment. The fall of even an enemy nation moves a compassionate heart.
Isaiah weeps for the wasted vine of Sibmah, his heart sounding 'like an harp for Moab.' Grief mingles with judgment. The fall of even an enemy nation moves a compassionate heart.

When Moab wearies herself on the high place, her prayers to idols fail; within three years her glory shall be despised and her remnant small. Her false worship cannot save. The day of her humbling is fixed and near.
